rationale: GT's substance is: (1) approve, core fix correctly restores codes.audio feedback matching #4527; (2) the new containment fallback silently drops the 'strict behavior preserved' guarantee for all callers, docstring is now stale; (3) minor note on legacy-vs-nested check order. Y independently verifies the writer/reader shape match (citing tts_postprocess:246 and stage_input_processors usage, matching GT's own reasoning) and its top non-blocking comment — containment fallback loosens ALL callers, with a concrete 'hello'/'hello world goodbye' failure example — is essentially the same discovery as GT's docstring-staleness comment, just framed as a behavioral risk rather than a doc fix. X's findings cluster around plausible-but-tangential nits (docstring on tts_preprocess rather than the actual stale docstring in assertions.py, magic numbers, CJK splitting) and includes a self-contradiction: its 'Validated' section calls the transcript_words guard 'reachable... correctly returns False' then later claims the same line is 'unreachable dead code,' undermining precision. Both give file:line-anchored, concrete suggestions, so actionability is comparable, but Y is clearly stronger on recall and precision.
